reason: This video is an educational tutorial demonstrating how to construct a low-cost laparoscopic box trainer using cardboard, not a performance or expert demonstration of an actual FLS task. No FLS task (peg transfer, pattern cut, endoloop, extracorporeal suture, or intracorporeal suture) is being performed.

video_content_summary.classification_rationale: The video contains multiple instructional slides about simulation training principles, ergonomics, port placement, and research papers, followed by demonstration frames showing assembly of a homemade cardboard box trainer. No actual FLS task execution is present.
slides_observed: Commercial vs proposed inanimate box trainer comparison
Smart phone camera capabilities for laparoscopic training
DIY box trainer assembly instruction frames
Purpose statement: making cheap, eco-friendly, personalized trainers
video_content_summary.demonstration_frames: Show cardboard box construction, port hole drilling, tape application, and setup with laparoscopic instruments and suturing materials, but no actual task performance

demonstrated_techniques: Port hole placement and spacing (60° angle, 16cm sides, 13.8cm center height)
Alternative visualization using smartphone/tablet instead of laparoscope
Ergonomic positioning based on research (optimal manipulation angle 60°)
verbal_cues: Why this video: to show how to make a box trainer that is cheap, eco-friendly, personalised, does not need a camera assistant and fun to make
To bring simulation training into your study room
common_errors_addressed: Port placement ergonomics: 60° manipulation angle maximizes efficiency
Commercial box trainer limitations: high cost, less personalization
Alternative to expensive laparoscope systems using mobile device cameras
teaching_content.target_audience: Medical students or surgical trainees seeking low-cost training equipment alternatives

scoring_not_applicable.explanation: FLS scoring rubrics apply only to trainee or expert performances of the five defined tasks. This video documents construction methodology and educational principles, not task execution. No completion time, penalties, knot quality, suture placement, or cutting accuracy can be assessed because no actual FLS task is performed.
